Wikidata name: Takenouchi Kaidō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:47, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Citation needed|date=March 2013|sortkey=道国166たけのうちかいとう}}
{{right| 
[[File:Takenouchi-Touge01.JPG|thumb|none|256px|{{ill|Takenouchi Pass|ja|竹内峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Pass|1=Takenouchi Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598871}} (at the Osaka-Nara prefectural border). The left side is part of {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166}}, while the right side shows the old highway.]]
}}
'''Takenouchi Highway''' (竹内街道) is the oldest ancient government road in Japan, stretching from {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}} to the area near {{ill|Nagao Shrine|en|Nagao Shrine|WD=Q6958481|lt=Nagao Shrine}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Nara|en|Katsuragi, Nara|WD=Q659672|lt=Katsuragi}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. Historically, it was known as '''Tajihi Road''' (丹比道).<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Osaka no Kaidō (大阪の街道)|year=1989|publisher=Shōraisha|page=66}}</ref>

From the {{ill|Shiratori Intersection|ja|白鳥交差点|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiratori Intersection|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Habikino|en|Habikino|WD=Q694308|lt=Habikino}} to the vicinity of the Takenouchi settlement in Katsuragi, much of the route was designated as {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166}} in 1974. In 2017, it became the first {{ill|Japan Heritage|en|Japan Heritage|WD=Q18382798|lt=Japan Heritage}} site in Osaka.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|title=Japan Heritage Recognition! Japan's Oldest National Route "Takenouchi Highway and Yokooji"|url=http://www.saikonokandou.com/2017/04/1470/|website=Saikonokandou.com|accessdate=2019-04-04}}</ref> Additionally, in {{ill|Taishi, Osaka|en|Taishi, Osaka|WD=Q1347173|lt=Taishi}}, major events such as the "Taishi Sacred Lantern Gathering" in spring and the "Takenouchi Highway Lantern Festival" in autumn are held annually.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.town.taishi.osaka.jp/event_info/nenkangyouji1/1534900110524.html|title=FY2018 Annual Events|website=Taishi Town Official Website|publisher={{ill|Taishi, Osaka|en|Taishi, Osaka|WD=Q1347173|lt=Taishi}}|accessdate=2019-04-07}}</ref>

== Overview ==

The Takenouchi Highway is an ancient arterial road that traverses the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}}, connecting {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} with {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}. It is regarded as Japan's oldest {{ill|ancient highway|ja|古代官道|1=kandō|lt=Ancient Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient highway|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官道|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452141}}. Extending approximately 26 kilometers from the Daishōji area of Sakai to Nagao Shrine in Katsuragi (23 kilometers within Osaka Prefecture), it retains remnants of its original path.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Rom International (ed.)|date=2005|title=Road Map: Amazing Knowledge Encyclopedia (道路location map びっくり!博学知識)|publisher=Kawade Shobō Shinsha|series=Kawade Yume Bunko|pages=52–53|isbn=4-309-49566-4}}</ref> Though the bustling cityscapes of Naniwa and Asuka have obscured its original width (once up to 30 meters during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}), the highway's historical significance as a transportation and cultural transmission route remains evident through its proximity to numerous ancient Kofuns, such as.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Asai|date=2001|title=Dictionary of Roads and Paths (道と路がわかる辞典)|publisher=Nihon Jitsugyō Shuppansha|isbn=4-534-03315-X}}</ref>

* '''{{ill|Kondayama Kofun|ja|誉田御廟山古墳|1=Konda Gobyoyama Kofun|lt=Emperor Ōjin's Mausoleum|ja|応神天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kondayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Konda Gobyoyama Kofun|qq=}}'''
* '''{{ill|Daisen Kofun|fi|Daisenry%C5%8D-kofun|pt|Daisenry%C5%8D-Kofun|fr|Kofun Daisenry%C5%8D|es|Daisenry%C5%8D-Kofun|en|Daisen Kofun|ca|Kofun Daisenry%C5%8D|ko|%EB%8B%A4%EC%9D%B4%EC%84%BC%EB%A6%AD %EA%B3%A0%EB%B6%84|zh|%E5%A4%A7%E4%BB%99%E9%99%B5%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3|ja|大仙陵古墳|1=Daisenryō Kofun|lt=Emperor Nintoku's Mausoleum|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisenryō Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}'''
* '''{{ill|Empress Suiko's Mausoleum|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|lt=Empress Suiko's Mausoleum (Yamada Takatsuka Kofun)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko's Mausoleum|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|qq=}}'''

The highway's name derives from the Takenouchi settlement near the {{ill|Takenouchi Pass|ja|竹内峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Pass|1=Takenouchi Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598871}}, located in Katsuragi.{{sfn|Kenji Asai|2001}}

== History ==

The Takenouchi Highway is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} under the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} in 613 CE. It describes the establishment of a "grand road" connecting Naniwa (modern {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}) to the capital in Asuka.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Asai|date=2001|title=道と路がわかる辞典|publisher=Nihon Jitsugyō Shuppansha}}</ref> One theory suggests that {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} commissioned the highway to facilitate diplomatic exchanges with the mainland, especially for envoys such as {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko}} during missions to the {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui dynasty}}.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Rom International (ed.)|date=2005|title=道路location map びっくり!博学知識|publisher=Kawade Shobō Shinsha}}</ref>

During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, the highway served as a conduit for the cultural and technological advancements brought by envoys and monks from the mainland. It played a pivotal role in transmitting the advanced culture and Buddhism that became foundational to Japanese civilization.{{sfn|Kenji Asai|2001}}

In the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=Middle Ages}}, it remained a vital route as part of the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}}. By 1890, it had been designated a prefectural road, and in 1974, it became part of {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166}}.{{sfn|Rom International (ed.)|2005|p=52}} Thus, the highway has been in continuous use from the Asuka period to the present.

== History ==

The Takenouchi Highway is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} under the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} in 613 CE. It describes the establishment of a "grand road" connecting Naniwa (modern {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}) to the capital in Asuka.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Asai|date=2001|title=道と路がわかる辞典|publisher=Nihon Jitsugyō Shuppansha}}</ref> One theory suggests that {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} commissioned the highway to facilitate diplomatic exchanges with the mainland, especially for envoys such as {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko}} during missions to the {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui dynasty}}.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Rom International (ed.)|date=2005|title=道路location map びっくり!博学知識|publisher=Kawade Shobō Shinsha}}</ref>

During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, the highway served as a conduit for the cultural and technological advancements brought by envoys and monks from the mainland. It played a pivotal role in transmitting the advanced culture and Buddhism that became foundational to Japanese civilization.{{sfn|Kenji Asai|2001}}

In the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=Middle Ages}}, it remained a vital route as part of the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}}. By 1890, it had been designated a prefectural road, and in 1974, it became part of {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166}}.{{sfn|Rom International (ed.)|2005|p=52}} Thus, the highway has been in continuous use from the Asuka period to the present.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:47, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Citation needed|date=March 2013|sortkey=道国166たけのうちかいとう}}
{{right| 
[[File:Takenouchi-Touge01.JPG|thumb|none|256px|{{ill|Takenouchi Pass|ja|竹内峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Pass|1=Takenouchi Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598871}} (at the Osaka-Nara prefectural border). The left side is part of {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166}}, while the right side shows the old highway.]]
}}
'''Takenouchi Highway''' (竹内街道) is the oldest ancient government road in Japan, stretching from {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}} to the area near {{ill|Nagao Shrine|en|Nagao Shrine|WD=Q6958481|lt=Nagao Shrine}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Nara|en|Katsuragi, Nara|WD=Q659672|lt=Katsuragi}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. Historically, it was known as '''Tajihi Road''' (丹比道).<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Osaka no Kaidō (大阪の街道)|year=1989|publisher=Shōraisha|page=66}}</ref>

From the {{ill|Shiratori Intersection|ja|白鳥交差点|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiratori Intersection|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Habikino|en|Habikino|WD=Q694308|lt=Habikino}} to the vicinity of the Takenouchi settlement in Katsuragi, much of the route was designated as {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166}} in 1974. In 2017, it became the first {{ill|Japan Heritage|en|Japan Heritage|WD=Q18382798|lt=Japan Heritage}} site in Osaka.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|title=Japan Heritage Recognition! Japan's Oldest National Route "Takenouchi Highway and Yokooji"|url=http://www.saikonokandou.com/2017/04/1470/|website=Saikonokandou.com|accessdate=2019-04-04}}</ref> Additionally, in {{ill|Taishi, Osaka|en|Taishi, Osaka|WD=Q1347173|lt=Taishi}}, major events such as the "Taishi Sacred Lantern Gathering" in spring and the "Takenouchi Highway Lantern Festival" in autumn are held annually.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.town.taishi.osaka.jp/event_info/nenkangyouji1/1534900110524.html|title=FY2018 Annual Events|website=Taishi Town Official Website|publisher={{ill|Taishi, Osaka|en|Taishi, Osaka|WD=Q1347173|lt=Taishi}}|accessdate=2019-04-07}}</ref>

== Overview ==

The Takenouchi Highway is an ancient arterial road that traverses the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}}, connecting {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} with {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}. It is regarded as Japan's oldest {{ill|ancient highway|ja|古代官道|1=kandō|lt=Ancient Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient highway|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官道|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452141}}. Extending approximately 26 kilometers from the Daishōji area of Sakai to Nagao Shrine in Katsuragi (23 kilometers within Osaka Prefecture), it retains remnants of its original path.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Rom International (ed.)|date=2005|title=Road Map: Amazing Knowledge Encyclopedia (道路location map びっくり!博学知識)|publisher=Kawade Shobō Shinsha|series=Kawade Yume Bunko|pages=52–53|isbn=4-309-49566-4}}</ref> Though the bustling cityscapes of Naniwa and Asuka have obscured its original width (once up to 30 meters during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}), the highway's historical significance as a transportation and cultural transmission route remains evident through its proximity to numerous ancient Kofuns, such as.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Asai|date=2001|title=Dictionary of Roads and Paths (道と路がわかる辞典)|publisher=Nihon Jitsugyō Shuppansha|isbn=4-534-03315-X}}</ref>

* '''{{ill|Kondayama Kofun|ja|誉田御廟山古墳|1=Konda Gobyoyama Kofun|lt=Emperor Ōjin's Mausoleum|ja|応神天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kondayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Konda Gobyoyama Kofun|qq=}}'''
* '''{{ill|Daisen Kofun|fi|Daisenry%C5%8D-kofun|pt|Daisenry%C5%8D-Kofun|fr|Kofun Daisenry%C5%8D|es|Daisenry%C5%8D-Kofun|en|Daisen Kofun|ca|Kofun Daisenry%C5%8D|ko|%EB%8B%A4%EC%9D%B4%EC%84%BC%EB%A6%AD %EA%B3%A0%EB%B6%84|zh|%E5%A4%A7%E4%BB%99%E9%99%B5%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3|ja|大仙陵古墳|1=Daisenryō Kofun|lt=Emperor Nintoku's Mausoleum|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisenryō Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}'''
* '''{{ill|Empress Suiko's Mausoleum|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|lt=Empress Suiko's Mausoleum (Yamada Takatsuka Kofun)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko's Mausoleum|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|qq=}}'''

The highway's name derives from the Takenouchi settlement near the {{ill|Takenouchi Pass|ja|竹内峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Pass|1=Takenouchi Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598871}}, located in Katsuragi.{{sfn|Kenji Asai|2001}}

== History ==

The Takenouchi Highway is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} under the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} in 613 CE. It describes the establishment of a "grand road" connecting Naniwa (modern {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}) to the capital in Asuka.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Asai|date=2001|title=道と路がわかる辞典|publisher=Nihon Jitsugyō Shuppansha}}</ref> One theory suggests that {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} commissioned the highway to facilitate diplomatic exchanges with the mainland, especially for envoys such as {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko}} during missions to the {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui dynasty}}.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Rom International (ed.)|date=2005|title=道路location map びっくり!博学知識|publisher=Kawade Shobō Shinsha}}</ref>

It played a pivotal role in transmitting the advanced culture and Buddhism that became foundational to Japanese civilization.{{sfn|Kenji Asai|2001}}

In the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=Middle Ages}}, it remained a vital route as part of the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}}. {{Citation needed|date=March 2013|sortkey=道国166たけのうちかいとう}}
{{right| 
[[File:Takenouchi-Touge01.JPG|thumb|none|256px|{{ill|Takenouchi Pass|ja|竹内峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Pass|1=Takenouchi Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598871}} (at the Osaka-Nara prefectural border). The left side is part of {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166}}, while the right side shows the old highway.]]
}}
'''Takenouchi Highway''' (竹内街道) is the oldest ancient government road in Japan, stretching from {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}} to the area near {{ill|Nagao Shrine|en|Nagao Shrine|WD=Q6958481|lt=Nagao Shrine}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Nara|en|Katsuragi, Nara|WD=Q659672|lt=Katsuragi}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. Historically, it was known as '''Tajihi Road''' (丹比道).<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Osaka no Kaidō (大阪の街道)|year=1989|publisher=Shōraisha|page=66}}</ref>

From the {{ill|Shiratori Intersection|ja|白鳥交差点|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiratori Intersection|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Habikino|en|Habikino|WD=Q694308|lt=Habikino}} to the vicinity of the Takenouchi settlement in Katsuragi, much of the route was designated as {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166}} in 1974. In 2017, it became the first {{ill|Japan Heritage|en|Japan Heritage|WD=Q18382798|lt=Japan Heritage}} site in Osaka.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|title=Japan Heritage Recognition! Japan's Oldest National Route "Takenouchi Highway and Yokooji"|url=http://www.saikonokandou.com/2017/04/1470/|website=Saikonokandou.com|accessdate=2019-04-04}}</ref> Additionally, in {{ill|Taishi, Osaka|en|Taishi, Osaka|WD=Q1347173|lt=Taishi}}, major events such as the "Taishi Sacred Lantern Gathering" in spring and the "Takenouchi Highway Lantern Festival" in autumn are held annually.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.town.taishi.osaka.jp/event_info/nenkangyouji1/1534900110524.html|title=FY2018 Annual Events|website=Taishi Town Official Website|publisher={{ill|Taishi, Osaka|en|Taishi, Osaka|WD=Q1347173|lt=Taishi}}|accessdate=2019-04-07}}</ref>

== Overview ==

The Takenouchi Highway is an ancient arterial road that traverses the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}}, connecting {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} with {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}. It is regarded as Japan's oldest {{ill|ancient highway|ja|古代官道|1=kandō|lt=Ancient Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient highway|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官道|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452141}}. Extending approximately 26 kilometers from the Daishōji area of Sakai to Nagao Shrine in Katsuragi (23 kilometers within Osaka Prefecture), it retains remnants of its original path.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Rom International (ed.)|date=2005|title=Road Map: Amazing Knowledge Encyclopedia (道路location map びっくり!博学知識)|publisher=Kawade Shobō Shinsha|series=Kawade Yume Bunko|pages=52–53|isbn=4-309-49566-4}}</ref> Though the bustling cityscapes of Naniwa and Asuka have obscured its original width (once up to 30 meters during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}), the highway's historical significance as a transportation and cultural transmission route remains evident through its proximity to numerous ancient Kofuns, such as.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Asai|date=2001|title=Dictionary of Roads and Paths (道と路がわかる辞典)|publisher=Nihon Jitsugyō Shuppansha|isbn=4-534-03315-X}}</ref>

* '''{{ill|Kondayama Kofun|ja|誉田御廟山古墳|1=Konda Gobyoyama Kofun|lt=Emperor Ōjin's Mausoleum|ja|応神天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kondayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Konda Gobyoyama Kofun|qq=}}'''
* '''{{ill|Daisen Kofun|fi|Daisenry%C5%8D-kofun|pt|Daisenry%C5%8D-Kofun|fr|Kofun Daisenry%C5%8D|es|Daisenry%C5%8D-Kofun|en|Daisen Kofun|ca|Kofun Daisenry%C5%8D|ko|%EB%8B%A4%EC%9D%B4%EC%84%BC%EB%A6%AD %EA%B3%A0%EB%B6%84|zh|%E5%A4%A7%E4%BB%99%E9%99%B5%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3|ja|大仙陵古墳|1=Daisenryō Kofun|lt=Emperor Nintoku's Mausoleum|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisenryō Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}'''
* '''{{ill|Empress Suiko's Mausoleum|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|lt=Empress Suiko's Mausoleum (Yamada Takatsuka Kofun)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko's Mausoleum|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|qq=}}'''

The highway's name derives from the Takenouchi settlement near the {{ill|Takenouchi Pass|ja|竹内峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Pass|1=Takenouchi Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598871}}, located in Katsuragi.{{sfn|Kenji Asai|2001}}

== History ==

The Takenouchi Highway is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} under the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} in 613 CE. It describes the establishment of a "grand road" connecting Naniwa (modern {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}) to the capital in Asuka.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Asai|date=2001|title=道と路がわかる辞典|publisher=Nihon Jitsugyō Shuppansha}}</ref> One theory suggests that {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} commissioned the highway to facilitate diplomatic exchanges with the mainland, especially for envoys such as {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko}} during missions to the {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui dynasty}}.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Rom International (ed.)|date=2005|title=道路location map びっくり!博学知識|publisher=Kawade Shobō Shinsha}}</ref>

During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, the highway served as a conduit for the cultural and technological advancements brought by envoys and monks from the mainland. It played a pivotal role in transmitting the advanced culture and Buddhism that became foundational to Japanese civilization.{{sfn|Kenji Asai|2001}}

In the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=Middle Ages}}, it remained a vital route as part of the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}}. By 1890, it had been designated a prefectural road, and in 1974, it became part of {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166}}.{{sfn|Rom International (ed.)|2005|p=52}} Thus, the highway has been in continuous use from the Asuka period to the present.

[[File:Takenouchi-Touge01.JPG|thumb|none|256px|{{ill|Takenouchi Pass|ja|竹内峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Pass|1=Takenouchi Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598871}} (at the Osaka-Nara prefectural border). The left side is part of {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166|1=Japan National Route 166}}, while the right side shows the old highway.]]
}}
'''Takenouchi Highway''' (竹内街道) is the oldest ancient government road in Japan, stretching from {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai|1=Sakai}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}} to the area near {{ill|Nagao Shrine|en|Nagao Shrine|WD=Q6958481|lt=Nagao Shrine|1=Nagao Shrine}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Nara|en|Katsuragi, Nara|WD=Q659672|lt=Katsuragi|1=Katsuragi, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. Historically, it was known as '''Tajihi Road''' (丹比道).<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Osaka no Kaidō (大阪の街道)|year=1989|publisher=Shōraisha|page=66}}</ref>

From the {{ill|Shiratori Intersection|ja|白鳥交差点|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiratori Intersection|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Habikino|en|Habikino|WD=Q694308|lt=Habikino|1=Habikino}} to the vicinity of the Takenouchi settlement in Katsuragi, much of the route was designated as {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166|1=Japan National Route 166}} in 1974. In 2017, it became the first {{ill|Japan Heritage|en|Japan Heritage|WD=Q18382798|lt=Japan Heritage|1=Japan Heritage}} site in Osaka.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|title=Japan Heritage Recognition! Japan's Oldest National Route "Takenouchi Highway and Yokooji"|url=http://www.saikonokandou.com/2017/04/1470/|website=Saikonokandou.com|accessdate=2019-04-04}}</ref> Additionally, in {{ill|Taishi, Osaka|en|Taishi, Osaka|WD=Q1347173|lt=Taishi|1=Taishi, Osaka}}, major events such as the "Taishi Sacred Lantern Gathering" in spring and the "Takenouchi Highway Lantern Festival" in autumn are held annually.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.town.taishi.osaka.jp/event_info/nenkangyouji1/1534900110524.html|title=FY2018 Annual Events|website=Taishi Town Official Website|publisher={{ill|Taishi, Osaka|en|Taishi, Osaka|WD=Q1347173|lt=Taishi|1=Taishi, Osaka}}|accessdate=2019-04-07}}</ref>

== Overview ==

The Takenouchi Highway is an ancient arterial road that traverses the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}}, connecting {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} with {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}. It is regarded as Japan's oldest {{ill|ancient highway|ja|古代官道|1=kandō|lt=Ancient Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient highway|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官道|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452141}}. Extending approximately 26 kilometers from the Daishōji area of Sakai to Nagao Shrine in Katsuragi (23 kilometers within Osaka Prefecture), it retains remnants of its original path.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Rom International (ed.)|date=2005|title=Road Map: Amazing Knowledge Encyclopedia (道路location map びっくり!博学知識)|publisher=Kawade Shobō Shinsha|series=Kawade Yume Bunko|pages=52–53|isbn=4-309-49566-4}}</ref> Though the bustling cityscapes of Naniwa and Asuka have obscured its original width (once up to 30 meters during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}), the highway's historical significance as a transportation and cultural transmission route remains evident through its proximity to numerous ancient Kofuns, such as.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Asai|date=2001|title=Dictionary of Roads and Paths (道と路がわかる辞典)|publisher=Nihon Jitsugyō Shuppansha|isbn=4-534-03315-X}}</ref>

* '''{{ill|Kondayama Kofun|ja|誉田御廟山古墳|1=Konda Gobyoyama Kofun|lt=Emperor Ōjin's Mausoleum|ja|応神天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kondayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Konda Gobyoyama Kofun|qq=}}'''
* '''{{ill|Daisen Kofun|fi|Daisenry%C5%8D-kofun|pt|Daisenry%C5%8D-Kofun|fr|Kofun Daisenry%C5%8D|es|Daisenry%C5%8D-Kofun|en|Daisen Kofun|ca|Kofun Daisenry%C5%8D|ko|%EB%8B%A4%EC%9D%B4%EC%84%BC%EB%A6%AD %EA%B3%A0%EB%B6%84|zh|%E5%A4%A7%E4%BB%99%E9%99%B5%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3|ja|大仙陵古墳|1=Daisenryō Kofun|lt=Emperor Nintoku's Mausoleum|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisenryō Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}'''
* '''{{ill|Empress Suiko's Mausoleum|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|lt=Empress Suiko's Mausoleum (Yamada Takatsuka Kofun)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko's Mausoleum|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|qq=}}'''

The highway's name derives from the Takenouchi settlement near the {{ill|Takenouchi Pass|ja|竹内峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Pass|1=Takenouchi Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598871}}, located in Katsuragi.{{sfn|Kenji Asai|2001}}

== History ==

The Takenouchi Highway is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} under the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} in 613 CE. It describes the establishment of a "grand road" connecting Naniwa (modern {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}) to the capital in Asuka.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Asai|date=2001|title=道と路がわかる辞典|publisher=Nihon Jitsugyō Shuppansha}}</ref> One theory suggests that {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} commissioned the highway to facilitate diplomatic exchanges with the mainland, especially for envoys such as {{ill|Ono no Imoko|en|Ono no Imoko|WD=Q1284072|lt=Ono no Imoko|1=Ono no Imoko}} during missions to the {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui dynasty|1=Sui dynasty}}.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Rom International (ed.)|date=2005|title=道路location map びっくり!博学知識|publisher=Kawade Shobō Shinsha}}</ref>

During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, the highway served as a conduit for the cultural and technological advancements brought by envoys and monks from the mainland. It played a pivotal role in transmitting the advanced culture and Buddhism that became foundational to Japanese civilization.{{sfn|Kenji Asai|2001}}

In the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=Middle Ages|1=Middle Ages}}, it remained a vital route as part of the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}}. By 1890, it had been designated a prefectural road, and in 1974, it became part of {{ill|Japan National Route 166|en|Japan National Route 166|WD=Q11421414|lt=Japan National Route 166|1=Japan National Route 166}}.{{sfn|Rom International (ed.)|2005|p=52}} Thus, the highway has been in continuous use from the Asuka period to the present.
